narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 29, you've worked hard to build your profile for law school, coming from a lower-middle income family and planning to attend full-time like most students. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 70.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold that led to your application's rejection—schools use this to prioritize candidates likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, meaning your grades that year placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers, far below the typical 5th or 6th decile. Your Year 1 decile of 1st had a similar strong negative impact, as it also showed early underperformance compared to the average student's mid-range standing. While your undergraduate GPA was close to the dataset average of 3.21 and nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, it wasn't enough to offset those weaker high school deciles. Your family income bracket actually helped a bit, pushing toward a pass prediction since many from similar backgrounds still succeed. Your LSAT score, though below the strong average of around 37, provided a modest positive influence toward passing, and your full-time enrollment plan aligned with the vast majority of successful students, offering a small boost. Overall, those low deciles in Years 1 and 3 stood out as the most influential drags compared to typical applicants who maintain higher rankings throughout. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome early hurdles with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your growth. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift the model's view in your favor. We're rooting for you to turn this around.
